title: Someone was in your server
problem: A colleague messages you — did you create a user called sysbackup? I did not touch it. You check and something feels very wrong. Investigate the server, find everything the attacker did, and lock it down before they come back.

setup.description: Plant evidence of a simulated breach. Run as root or with sudo.
setup.note: This creates a fake attacker user with planted history, a backdoor cron job and a malicious script in /tmp. Nothing actually calls out to the internet — the URLs are fake.

hints: Start with who is on this server — cat /etc/passwd | grep -v nologin will show real shell users, then check sudo group members with getent group sudo
Check auth logs to see when sysbackup was created and if it logged in — grep sysbackup /var/log/auth.log on Ubuntu or /var/log/secure on CentOS
Attackers always leave crons and files behind — check crontab -l -u sysbackup and hunt for recently modified files with find /tmp /var/tmp -mtime -7 -type f 2>/dev/null
solution:
1. Find suspicious users:     cat /etc/passwd | grep -v nologin  # show users with real shells
2. Check sudo members:        getent group sudo  # sysbackup should not be here
3. Check login history:       last sysbackup  # see if and when they logged in
4. Check auth logs (Ubuntu):  grep sysbackup /var/log/auth.log  # when was account created
5. Check auth logs (CentOS):  grep sysbackup /var/log/secure  # same but CentOS path
6. Read their history:        sudo cat /home/sysbackup/.bash_history  # what commands did they run
7. Check their cron:          sudo crontab -l -u sysbackup  # look for backdoor cron jobs
8. Hunt planted files:        find /tmp /var/tmp -mtime -7 -type f 2>/dev/null  # recently modified files
9. Read the script:           cat /tmp/.rootkit.sh  # understand what it does before deleting
10. Delete the script:        sudo rm /tmp/.rootkit.sh  # remove the backdoor
11. Remove sudo access:       sudo gpasswd -d sysbackup sudo  # strip elevated privileges
12. Lock the account:         sudo usermod -L sysbackup  # disable login immediately
13. Expire the account:       sudo chage -E 0 sysbackup  # force account expiry
14. Delete entirely:          sudo userdel -r sysbackup  # remove user and home directory
15. Verify clean:             getent group sudo && last sysbackup  # confirm sudo clean and no new logins
